title: Kiss, Marry, Kill with Riesling: How Could You?
summary:
475 Words (Or So) on How One of Riesling’s Top Terroirs is Actually in Italy

The post Kiss, Marry, Kill with Riesling: How Could You? appeared first on Opening a Bottle.
content:
3 min read

On a recent trip to Alto Adige with a group of fellow wine professionals, the conversation shifted to a game of Kiss, Marry, Kill — a hypothetical game where three people are proposed, and you have to say which one you’d kiss, marry and — yes — kill. Kinda morbid, no? But of course, the five of us being wine nerds, we were playing the game with wine grapes: Chardonnay, Sauvignon Blanc and Riesling.

Riesling has big demands, and if they’re not met, it won’t work. You could say that somms love a diva, but really, they just appreciate another discerning individual.

These somms were all lovely people, and we became wonderful friends by trip’s end. But this game seemed anticlimactic from the start. Everybody was going to either kiss or marry Riesling.

Somms love Riesling, yet it seems like the more general consumers ignore this grape and its various regions, the more professionals are determined to promote it. To make it their personal mission to sell it by the glass, to write articles espousing its virtues, to bestow upon the world their insights on what makes “petrol and pears” so majestic.

My turn at the table arrived. “Marry Chardonnay, kiss Sauvignon Blanc, kill Riesling,” I said. The outrage was palpable. How could you? I just laughed, pleased with myself for finding everyone’s button to push by night one.

But I Love Riesling, I Really Do

There is no way to choose in such a silly game — you’re not killing these grapes off as much as you’re killing off Chablis, or Steiermark, or the Mosel. And truth is, I adore and respect Riesling as much as any grape in the world. When done right, it has the persistence, complexity and age-worthiness to match any wine — white or red.

But the wine professional’s love for Riesling goes deeper than that. Riesling has big demands, and if they’re not met, it won’t work. You could say that somms love a diva, but really, they just appreciate another discerning individual. Riesling requires a cool climate (highs in the 60s Fahrenheit, ideally) and it wants a big diurnal temperature range on the daily. And its happiest habitats are well-draining soils with tons of sunlight.

In other words, the highlands of Alto Adige, where we were playing this game. More specifically, the beautiful Valle Isarco, nestled against the Austrian border.

For years, I’ve written about Abbazia di Novacella, and espoused the wines of Cantina Valle Isarco and the family estate of Pacher Hof. Kuenhof is relatively new to me, but they make an extraordinary Riesling called “Kalton” which walks a razor’s edge from start to finish. It’s a finely honed blade of dynamic acidity, generous flavor and stony persistence. Kiss, Marry, Kill? Definitely not kill. Who would dare say such a thing?

2022 Kuenhof “Kalton” Südtirol-Alto Adige Eisacktaler Riesling

Südtirol-Alto Adige DOC (Trentino-Alto Adige )
Grapes: Riesling (100%)
Alcohol: 13%
Food-friendliness: Impeccable
Value: Very Good

     

A beginner might like … Riesling! That has to be the most obvious one of these I’ve ever written, but for real, I know very few casual wine drinkers in America who would accept a glass of Riesling (still!). Yet if there were a glass to change prejudices hearts and minds, its this effort from Kuenhof.

A wine obsessive might like … exploring how Riesling is the world’s greatest vessel for the sensation of minimality. The sensation — a stony persistence that usually can be attributed to well-draining soils — takes on new heights in the high elevation vineyards of the Dolomites.

 

Note: This wine was provided as a sample by the winery’s U.S. importer. Learn more about our editorial policy.

title: First-Taste Guide to Vouvray
summary:
Within This Historic AOC, Chenin Blanc’s Spectrum is on Full Display

The post First-Taste Guide to Vouvray appeared first on Opening a Bottle.
content:
9 min read

Think of the producers of Vouvray as painters and Chenin Blanc as every medium of paint at once: their tray of watercolors, their palette dotted in oils, their wells of tempera to dab a brush into. In Vouvray, this iconic grape explores its broadest range of possibilities, from sparkling wines that rival Champagne to ultra-sweet moelleux tinged with noble rot. Add to this gallery a collection of complex dry whites and you might start to think that Chenin Blanc can do anything it wants here.

If only it were that easy.

“It’s not an easy grape to grow, and it’s not a grape that you can express well everywhere,” says 13th-generation winemaker, Céline Champalou, who recently took time out of monitoring the bud break to discuss the Vouvray appellation for this guide. “Chenin Blanc needs a little bit of water. Here in Vouvray, with the clay, we can always keep the water, even if conditions are dry.”

With that most essential demand met, Chenin Blanc becomes fairly malleable to the producer’s intentions. “So you get the terroir, and then you have the grower,” she adds with a laugh. “You can put ten of us in a room with a few bottles of Vouvray and we could speak all the night about our different visions on why we love Vouvray, and how we express it.”

We’ll save that entertaining roundtable with the vignerons for another day. For now, let’s get you up to speed with the basics — as well as some first-taste suggestions — on this legacy appellation that always meets the moment.

3 Reasons to Try Vouvray

  1. You Are Intrigued with Chenin Blanc – France, California, South Africa … Compelling examples of this grape abound. But to fully understand this grape, you need to go to its roots in the Loire Valley. And no other AOC here has put the stylistic stamp on Chenin Blanc as much as Vouvray.
  2. You Are a French Wine Student – Then knowing Vouvray is as essential as Sancerre, Chablis and Champagne.
  3. You Want to See a Grape Served in All Its Forms – Perhaps the most exciting reason to uncork Vouvray lies in all the comparisons you can pour. What Vouvray lacks in singular style it makes up for in thrilling variety.

What is Vouvray?

The Vouvray AOC is devoted to Chenin Blanc through and through. Across eight different communes straddling both the left and right bank of the Loire River, the zone celebrates this unique grape in a variety of styles.

  • Vouvray AOC – Devoted to still wines in Sec (less than 8g/L RS), Demi-Sec (8–35g/L), Moelleux (greater than 35g/L RS) and Doux (greater than 150g/L RS) forms.
  • Vouvray Pétillant AOC – Devoted to ancestral method, lightly sparkling wines (aka pét-nat). This category requires a minimum of 12 months of lees aging before release.
  • Vouvray Mousseux AOC – Devoted to traditional-method sparkling wines. Like Pétillant, Mousseux also requires a minimum of 12 months of lees aging before release.

Pétillant examples are a little hard to find in the United States, so for purposes of this guide, we’ll focus on what’s most commercially available: the dry, semi-dry and fully sparkling versions. Think of these three as “Classic Chenin,” “Sweet and Rare Chenin” and “Vivacious Chenin.” We’ll touch upon each of them below.

About the Appellation and Its Wines

Vouvray lives and dies on Chenin Blanc. In today’s world, where climate change rewrites the rules constantly, and where trends change faster than vines can grow, going “all in” on one grape might be seen as risky business. But the chief reason Vouvray has stayed constant since its founding as an appellation in 1936 lies in its adaptability and versatility. One grape can be many things.

“Vouvray has always had a nice reputation,” says Céline Champalou of the eponymous Vouvray estate. “But people now want to know more about Chenin.”

While their grape may be having a moment, there is something about Vouvray that feels immune to trendiness. “It’s never experienced the craziness [of trends], but it’s also never had a falling reputation. It’s always here. It’s a sure value.”

The History

Viticulture in the area dates back to the 4th century, when monks at the Marmoutier Abbey planted grapes. Chenin Blanc emerged in the 9th century, when the first records of Pineau de la Loire — the grape’s historical name — appeared in local records. French royalty developed an insatiable thirst for the local wines in the 15th and 16th centuries, but it was the arrival of Dutch merchants in the 16th and 17th centuries that the name of “Vouvray” made it onto the wines. The Dutch saw potential in shipping and selling these wines to the key markets they served. So they organized local growers and blended the wines from across the Touraine region into a unified, marketable wine they called Vouvray after the small town at the heart of the region. The Dutch also introduced stabilizing techniques to winemaking, such as the addition of sulfur, so that the product wouldn’t spoil on its long journey to market. Vouvray soon became synonymous with quality wine in the cities of Rotterdam, Paris and London.

Quality and variety would be an ever-evolving storyline with the region. The underground infrastructure for aging wines was also already in place, thanks to the numerous quarries that provided stone for the many grand chateaux of the Loire Valley. And in the 18th and 19th century, Vouvray’s caves proved to be ideal for the aging of sparkling wine made in the méthode champenoise. From this point forward, Vouvray’s identity would be tied to numerous styles: the historically celebrated moelleux, the emerging mousseux, and the style that would become preferred in modern times, sec, or dry white wines.

Shortly after the Appellation d’Origine Contrôlée system was established in 1935 and then awarded in 1936, Vouvray earned its protected status from the French government. While it wasn’t part of the original six regions listed, it joined a groundswell movement across France that included the likes of Chablis, Saint-Émilion and Hermitage. But Vouvray was unique in this group for protecting multiple styles of wine from a single grape grown in a singular area — a hallmark of the region that has endured to this day.

The Grape & Terroir

Over our hour-long conversation, Céline Champalou personified her local grape variety in a way that only familiarity and adoration can. “The Chenin will take from his growers,” she said at one point. “For my side, I like elegance and fineness. But there are some friends who love Chenin with more shoulder, more power. They can do it on the same soil.”

When compared to other Loire Valley appellations that produce Chenin Blanc, Champalou feels that Saumur is most similar to Vouvray on the dry style of wines, and that Montlouis-Sur-Loire — just across the river — mirrors Vouvray’s creaminess. But even then, she feels Vouvray has a dimensionality to it that is unique.

“The difference is the background of the calcaire,” she says, referring to the limestone-based soil found across the region. “This gives us the mineral tension. But with our clay, too, we have a roundness, we have creaminess, we have fineness. I think in Vouvray maybe we have more elegance, and our power here is that we can have extraordinary balance.”

The Winemaking

One of the critical reasons that Vouvray has such an abundant variety of styles is the long window of maturation in the vineyards, which gives producers many options for harvest. Interested in making a sparkling wine? Producers can harvest early. Looking to make a richer, more structured still white wine? Give it a few weeks.

“Sometimes people just know one style,” says Champalou, noting that despite a surge of interest in varietal Chenin Blanc globally, the region’s sparkling wines are still the most famous. “There are many people who say, ‘Oh, Vouvray! Nice bubbles.’ And I pour them a still, dry wine. ‘Oh, this is not Vouvray,’ they’ll say. But yes, it is!”

But Champalou notes that the spirit of the winegrowers is often best felt when a little residual sugar is involved in the wines. A touch of sugar doesn’t have to mean sweetness; when done right, it can lend nuance and support more delicate tones in the wine. Throughout my tastings, I was surprised to learn some of the grams-per-liter figures on wines I assumed were dry. Often, the feeling of sugar seemed negligible in the range of 10g/L to 20 g/L of RS, because the character of Chenin Blanc’s acidity created a momentum that had my senses looking elsewhere.

The best of the lot was Champalou’s 2024 “Lieu-Dit Les Fondraux,” which I’ve profiled below. The wine offered a zestiness and citric appeal that was countered by rich pear-like fruit, and interesting herbaceousness for depth.

“With Les Fondraux, I make a promise to myself to always [retain some sugar], because this is the identity of Vouvray,” Champalou said when I asked her about the wine’s particulars. “Of course you can imagine, it is less easy to sell than a still dry wine. But when people taste it blind, they always love it. So we need to continue to educate people with this balance.”

“When you use certain words, if you speak about sugar, the mind is closed, and that’s it.”

Finding These Wines

Vouvray is a relatively easy wine to find across the American market. Each year, the AOC produces between 15 to 18 million bottles of wine across all categories. While I couldn’t track down specific numbers on what is exported to the United States, we remain one of their top markets. But based on recent region-wide data, it is safe to assume that each year, more than a million bottles make it to our shores.

That said, I always recommend prioritizing smaller family producers from European appellations, not only because of their feel-good story, but because of the personal stakes they have in quality. If they want to import their wines here, it has to hit the mark for importers, merchants and restaurateurs. To get through that gauntlet given the wine business’ current struggles, they have to meet high standards.



So Which Wines Shall You Try?

Ready to uncork some bottles of Vouvray? My wine recommendations leave pointless point scores aside, and instead give you a compelling reason for buying each wine, no matter where you are on your wine journey.

If you join my community of curious beginners and passionate wine obsessives, you’ll unlock context-rich tasting reports, wine recommendations, and live and recorded wine classes, all focused on Italy and other European wine countries.

Your First Taste of Vouvray

So what should Vouvray taste like at each of its categorical levels? As our guest winemaker alludes, there isn’t one set style even within a category like sec, and vintage variation sets the rules on just how far the boundaries are for a winemaker to place a stylistic stamp on the wine.

That said, there is a baseline with Chenin Blanc that should make it stand out from Chardonnay, Pinot Blanc, Grenache Blanc and certainly Sauvignon Blanc. The grape is noticeably higher in acidity than most white wine grapes, which is a trait amplified in Vouvray’s terroir. Its thick skins are not only durable enough to delay botrytis, they are loaded with phenolics, which can more fully mature in Vouvray’s extended harvest window. Expect sensations akin to ripe yellow fruits, tea leaves or chamomile, and most intriguingly, a trio of tertiary tones that can resemble ginger, beeswax and/or wet wool.

Lastly, Chenin Blanc’s sugar content — while not necessarily high or low — is neatly kept in check by the acidity and phenolics of the grapes, allowing for balance no matter how high it gets. This is especially true in Vouvray, and it results in the tapestry of styles profiled below.

For me, I seek out these traits in Vouvray no matter the category. Vouvray’s dry wines offer a fascinating display of flavors, and often a mineral sensation on the finish that accentuates persistence. The demi-sec and sweeter styles are all about exuberance and generosity: a spectrum of Chenin Blanc’s potential sensations balanced by its naturally high acidity. And speaking of acidity, that’s the hallmark for me of its sparkling wines. But in addition to the aromas and flavors of the traditional-method process, which can take precedence with neutral grapes like Chardonnay, you get the personality of Chenin Blanc shining through.

See if you can find these wines below, or one’s made from similar, small-production, family-owned wineries. I suspect you’ll have a lot of notes to jot down.

2024 Champalou Vouvray

       

Champalou is one of the more compelling wineries in the area, prizing site expression and aromatic intensity to create a set of Vouvray wines that feel intimate yet typical of the region. You are most likely to find a bottle of their entry-level Vouvray, a wine that sets a near-perfect baseline on what the sec category should be.

A beginner might like … searching for Chenin Blanc’s signature aromatic notes to commit to memory. For me, it often registers as similar to pears, chamomile and wool.

A wine obsessive might like … the interplay between freshness and joviality on one hand, and complexity and depth on the other.

2022 François et Julien Pinon Vouvray

         

Established in 1786, this remarkable winery has carried the torch for Vouvray over seven generations. They’re been certified organic since 2003, and their standard bearing Vouvray perfectly shows the potential for vivid, show-stopping flavors in the region’s wines.

A beginner might like … getting to know the richer side of Chenin Blanc with this show of stone-fruit, orange citrus and golden floral notes.

A wine obsessive might like … the mineral sensation on the finish, which lends persistence and entices a second sip.

2024 Champalou “Lieu-Dit Les Fondraux” Vouvray

         

Lieu-Dit Les Fondraux is a singular site with mixed clay and silex soils, which seems to foster a Chenin Blanc fruit with power and precision. The Champalou family has always taken the approach of leaving some residual sugar in this wine — roughly 20g/L — to balance its sharpness, making it a great first taste of the demi-sec style.

A beginner might like … buckling up for this ride. If Champalou’s Vouvray (listed above) profiles the typical aromas and textures of Chenin Blanc from this region, Lieu-Dit Les Fondraux reveals how those elements shape-shift over a glass. This wine is lively, complex and relentless in its persistence, and you hardly notice the residual sugar at all.

A wine obsessive might like … charting back vintages of this wine over several years. How does Vouvray age over time, and how does the demi-sec style give it even more endurance? Does it muffle the evolution of the notes or lend them a more profound ring?

NV Vignoble Alain Robert “Troglodyte” Vouvray Brut

     

While Vignoble Alain Robert makes dry Vouvray, they are best known for sparkling wines, especially “Troglodyte,” which not only takes its name from the cave-dwelling people of the Loire River Valley’s past, but the nickname of the wrens that live in the area’s limestone cliffs. The wine has zero dosage and spends up to 24 months on the lees.

A beginner might like … comparing this wine to a nonvintage Crémant d’Alsace, Crémant de Bourgogne and Champagne. I tend to look toward the width of the palate in such comparisons: how direct or how expansive does the fruit and acidity feel?

A wine obsessive might like … tracking the tertiary tones on this wine, which feel nuanced and worthy of discussion with fellow drinkers.

 

Note: Wines tasted and reviewed for this article were a mixture of free samples provided by the Vouvray AOC, and select wine importing partners, as well as three wines purchased for exploration (one of which, François et Julien Pinon Vouvray, made the cut in this article). title: 5 Reasons You Should Pay Attention to the Wines of Catalonia
summary:
We Could List Off 5 Great Appellations, But Let’s Look at the Foundations Instead

The post 5 Reasons You Should Pay Attention to the Wines of Catalonia appeared first on Opening a Bottle.
content:
7 min read

This past week, I led a masterclass on the wines of Catalonia as part of a wine-trade promotional event here in Denver, called Taste the Difference. They needed a local wine educator who knew Spanish wines, and I was their guy. While researching my talk and re-acquainting myself with Catalonia’s wines, I realized something — we really ought to cover this region more here on Opening a Bottle.

Why? Well, because of Spain’s 17 autonomous regions, Catalonia is certainly among the most fascinating and distinctive. While some regions are dominated by red wines, Catalonia offers everything: white, rosé, orange, red, sweet and of course, sparkling. Just within this last category, there are dozens of different styles and innovations. The Catalans don’t hold back on anything. There is a sort of full-throttle spirit to their explorations of taste, and I want to be a part of it more frequently.

But furthermore, Catalonia seems designed for vinous diversity. While some appellations might ebb and flow, the region as a whole seems impervious to stasis. I don’t think I’ll ever get bored with it.

So let’s open the floodgate here with these simple reasons you should be paying attention to Catalonia’s wines.

1. They’re Progressive and Forward-Thinking

Gaudí. Dalí. Adrià. Alexia. The modern history of this region is punctuated by Catalans who have made an impact on the world with their creativity, ingenuity and foresight. It doesn’t take long poking around this region to see the same drive infused into the wines.

Innovation in wine is often tagged to technology or adopting something old in a modern light. Catalonia abounds in both realms. In the former, one need look no further than the “space race” happening between Cava and Corpinnat. And while the natural wine narrative feels pretty tired in most places, Catalonia’s minimal interventionists are truly rewriting the reputations of such places as Penedès, Alella, Terra Alta and even Priorat.

But examples of this ingenuity don’t have to be as out-of-left-field as The Persistence of Memory. They can be as simple as Llopart’s decision to use 100% Monastrell in their NV Corpinnat Rosé Brut, which shimmers with a superb varietal character of plums and spice. Anytime a grape variety’s flavor identity can survive the traditional method process, I take note, and a recent tasting of this wine was a profound experience for me.

2. Their Modern Form is Rooted in an Extensive Viticultural History

As you might expect, wine culture in Catalonia is quite ancient. From Phoenician traders and Greek colonists to Roman legions who planted vines to hydrate their imperial ambitions, the grapevine has been threaded throughout much of Catalonia’s history. But that’s not unusual in the Mediterranean. What is unusual is how well this translates into modern contexts.

The city of Tarragona was the hub for Roman development and a key transit point for their wine trade. In the shadow of its ancient Roman ruins, you can find several compelling bodegas to sample the local scene.

Many of the stone terraces in Priorat and Montsant were originally constructed by Carthusian monks beginning in the 12th century. What seemed ordinary to them is now deemed heroic in today’s wine parlance, but the fact remains that the best wines from the area often come from the steepest slopes that we wouldn’t dream of cultivating today.

The historical threads also carry over to winemaking techniques, with the revival of amphora usage and traditional skin-contact white wines which carry the preservative power of tannins and polyphenols (not to mention exceptional flavor intensity). So fundamental is this technique that the DO Terra Alta created the brisat category under its appellation laws, and in so doing, became the first PDO in Europe to sanction and codify an orange wine technique.

3. They Embrace Native Grape Varieties

I will also point to Italian wine as my personal North Star for wine appreciation. And maybe that’s why I relate so much to Catalonia’s wines: few regions in Spain can compare to its embrace of diverse native grape varieties.

While Cava may be seen as a pop star in the Catalan firmament, it actually supports this claim quite well. Across Europe, similar traditional-method sparkling wine regions have emulated Champagne’s grapes, particularly Chardonnay and Pinot Noir. While Cava owes a great deal of its initial inspiration to Champagne, the grapes have stayed local: Macabeo, Parellada and the most compelling, Xarel·lo.

I recently met with Joan Cusiné Carol of the celebrated DO Cava and DO Penedès estate Parés Baltà, and he demonstrated to me how Xarel·lo exudes “main character energy” with his family’s single vineyard wine, the 2023 “Electio,” a 100% Xarel·lo that sees four hours of maceration and barrel aging. Its creamy core, nutty highlights and superb persistence made it one of the more memorable white wines I’ve had this summer.

On the red side of things, Garnatxa Negra (Grenache) remains king, and while its origin story is tied to neighboring Aragón, the grape is considered local to Catalonia and has borne the hallmarks of place for roughly 800 years.

4. They Are Often Organic (For What That’s Worth)

I’m no absolutist, but I do believe that wine regions with larger organic footprints tend to have a brighter future. Yes, there is a valid debate about organic producers’ frequent reliance on copper, but on the whole, organically treated vineyards offer a better environment for preserving and fostering more biodiversity, which in the end is vitally important to the next generation of agriculture.

Catalonia is rich with certified organic estates. In fact, the DO Penedès — all 160 wineries and 4,509 hectares of it — has mandated that the entire vineyard surface be organic. The 2025 harvest was the first fully organic wine harvest in a sanctioned wine region in the world. Priorat, Montsant and Empordà can also lay claim to high amounts of organic production, as can some of the smaller DO’s like Alella.

One recent wine that I’ve written about that encapsulates this movement well is Anna Espelt’s “Pla de Gates” Empordà Garnatxa Blanco.

5. They Still Haven’t Fully Caught On … But Should

With Ferran Adrià’s deconstructed of cuisine as its catalyst, Catalonia’s restaurants have done more to reshape global fine dining over the last 30 years than any region on earth. Yet its equally distinctive wine industry has yet to command the premium prices its quality deserves.

But you know that the world doesn’t need? More “premiumized” wines. Mercifully, Catalonia’s wines are still quite affordable. And increasingly, they’re punching above their weight.

Cava is slowly shaking off its reputation as grocery store wine, partly because of its emerging rival, Corpinnat — an artisanal band of former Cava producers who demand only hand-harvested organic grapes from indigenous varieties. The buzz around Corpinnat has pushed Cava to up its game, and the Consejo Regulador has ultimately focused on the right initiatives to improve quality, such as better defined aging tiers and demands from producers to go fully organic. Yet even with all these positive changes, both categories of sparkling wine are between $20 and $40. Absurdly low given how labor-intensive such wines are.

As one of only two DOQs in Spain (the highest level of appellation), Priorat commands a hefty price, but even compared to similar age-worthy, structured reds from France, Italy and the USA, it is far from exorbitant. Meanwhile, bargains abound in Montsant, Terra Alta, Costers del Segre, Empordà and Penedès for expressive, compelling and satisfying reds and whites. In an age when even entry-level Barbera tilts over $30/bottle — and entry-level Champagne starts at $50 — price-to-quality is the only reason you need to seek out the wines of Catalonia.

 

Beyond Opening a Bottle

If you love the wines of Catalonian already and want to dive deeper into the latest trends and happenings in the region, I have two highly opinionated, B.S. free websites to recommend.

One would be Jason Wilson’s Substack, Everyday Drinking. Wilson spends a considerable amount of his time in Spain, often in wine bars where the true zeitgeist can be felt and interpreted. He and I spent an evening bar hopping in Florence two years ago, and it was one of the most thought-provoking nights of wine discussion I’ve had with a fellow journalist.

The other is Hudin.com, a paywall website for Spanish wine fanatics run by Miquel Hudin, who is based in Priorat and regularly issues comprehensive tasting reports on Catalonia’s wines.

title: What Garnacha Blanca Has to Say About Mediterranean Influence in Wine
summary:
450 Words (Or So) on an Inspired White Wine from Empordà

The post What Garnacha Blanca Has to Say About Mediterranean Influence in Wine appeared first on Opening a Bottle.
content:
3 min read

The phrase “Mediterranean influence” can mean a lot of things. If you step outside a wine mindset, you’d likely think of cuisine, and how olive oil, lemons and fresh fish might change the narrative of a dish. Or I suppose it might conjure an image of a lavish vacation. Sorry I missed your call. I was under the influence of the Mediterranean, somewhere between Cannes and Portofino.

If a wine can conjure this much sense of place, it belongs in your glass.

Personally, I can’t get out of a wine mindset with this term, because I hear it constantly mentioned by any winery seemingly within a two-hour drive of this great sea. It always reads like an equation: warmth + mild temperature swings + salty sea breezes = saline, balanced wines.

Or at least, that’s the impression people want to make when they tout their product.

In the case of Anna Espelt and her Mas Marés vineyards in Catalonia’s DO Empordà, the phrase might as well be archaeological. Located in the Parque Natural del Cap de Creus near the village of Cadaqués, this vineyard is the easternmost in mainland Spain, and the open, wind-beaten landscape of the headland has been a thoroughfare for human activity since the Bronze Age. Archaeological artifacts lay everywhere, including the Dolmen de la Creu d’en Cobertella which dates back to 3500 BCE. Looking like a cross between Stonehenge and a Coleman tent, it is simultaneously a reminder of Catalonia’s long arc of history and the relentlessness of the area’s winds. Need shelter? These 800-pound slabs of rock ought to do. (More recently, Cadaqués native son Salvador Dalí depicted the landscape of Cap de Creus behind the melting clocks in his most famous work, The Persistence of Memory).

Wind might be the most important factor in “Mediterranean influence.” It fills sails and moves people, and prompts them to hunker down and find shelter or a calm port, and it benefits their crops. In the case of wine grapes — specifically Garnacha Blanca and Garnacha Gris — the wind forces the vines to produce smaller, more flavorful berries, and to lean-in to acidity production. “Pla de Gates” is one of the most riveting Spanish white wines I’ve had in years. It opens with a salvo of tropical fruit and roasted, nutty aromas, then moves across the palate with the casual grace of a Mediterranean holiday. A tinge of bitterness on the finish reminds you that not everything is easy or jolly, but in that sharp edge you get a sense of character, and it doesn’t quickly leave the palate. It lingers with a salty persistence. You want to go back for another sip.

If a wine can conjure this much sense of place, it belongs in your glass.

2023 Anna Espelt  “Pla de Gates” Empordà Blanc

Empordà DO (Catalonia )
Grapes: Garnacha Blanca (80%), Garnacha Gris (20%)
Alcohol: 13%
Food friendliness: Selective
Price: Very Good

     

A beginner might like … imaging the wild vineyard where this white blend hails from while planning a vacation to Barcelona and the Catalan countryside. If ever there was a wine for “arm chair travel …”

A wine obsessive might like … knowing a little bit more about Garnacha Gris, a pink-skinned mutation of Grenache that lends aromas and richness to the cuvée when blended with Garnacha Blanca. The grape is best known for its use just over the border in France, where it often plays an important supporting role in the world-class fortified sweet wine known as Banyuls.

title: The Breath of Life in the Wines of Manincor
summary:
Sometimes on the wine road, you encounter a figure who seems to have it all figured out — life, nature, balance. They saunter from topic to topic with ease, and proclaim that they drink wine everyday as they look upon their land with a beatific gaze. Italy is riddled with these “mascots of the good life,” and they’re often full of B.S. But up north along the border with Austria — in the region known by the dual name of […]

The post The Breath of Life in the Wines of Manincor appeared first on Opening a Bottle.
content:
8 min read

Sometimes on the wine road, you encounter a figure who seems to have it all figured out — life, nature, balance. They saunter from topic to topic with ease, and proclaim that they drink wine everyday as they look upon their land with a beatific gaze. Italy is riddled with these “mascots of the good life,” and they’re often full of B.S. But up north along the border with Austria — in the region known by the dual name of Südtirol-Alto Adige — they are few and far between. The region may be “the warmest place in the Germanic world,” as one producer told me on a recent visit, but that is merely a climatic descriptor. Culturally, they are still very much Germanic: direct, technical, and while subtly friendly, not at all inclined to myth making.

“We are really blessed here. Really truly blessed.”

Michael Count Goëss-Enzenberg
Owner, Manincor

So when I met Michael Count Goëss-Enzenberg of Manincor a few weeks ago along with a group of sommeliers, the skeptical journalist in me was surprised to feel immediately disarmed by his radiance and warmth.

He was tall, thin, and while smartly dressed — this guy seems to live in a blazer and chinos, I wrote in my notes — he was intimately familiar with the toil of making memorable wine. Those chinos might not see many dirt stains, but he genuinely cared about the massive amount of natural resources he had inherited through his noble line. He made sure our group knew he knew that.

“We are really blessed here,” Michael said. “Really truly blessed.”

The turquoise waters of the Kalterersee shimmered nearby, inviting a swim even in spring. There was a castle crowning a hill above the lake, and the dramatic quartz porphyry cliffs of the Alps carved the facing horizon line to the west.

But Michael had his eye on a distant grove of forest that draped down the slope near the castle. His family has long owned those woods, but as with any resource, it has to be responsibly managed. For years, they have selectively harvested oak to provide sunlight for the next generation of plants and trees. When done right, the process can promote biodiversity and resilience of the ecosystem, and it can give you a handy ingredient in winemaking.

“About 30 percent of the oak in our barrels comes from our forest,” Michael noted. The staves are seasoned for three years at Manincor, then provided to the acclaimed Austrian barrel maker Stockinger, who incorporates them into various sized barrels. “But we have to be careful with our forest,” he added. “It takes 100 years to regenerate.”

That far-horizon mindset — thinking in centuries, not vintages — became a theme of Michael’s tour of Manincor. And because of that, there was a lack of do-gooderism at Manincor. This wasn’t about creating a green policy for marketing his product; it was about self-sufficiency. It seemed like the last thing Michael wanted to allow for was a squandering of the natural resources he had inherited.

Story continues below

My Award-Winning Book

Discover more stories about Italian wine with my full-color book, Opening a Bottle: Italy, which recently earned me the honor of Author of the Year from the Wine Travel Awards.

E-Book

Signed Copy (U.S. Only)

From Wilting to Flourishing

That was especially the case with his vineyards. He’d learned the hard way how delicate nature’s balance can be.

A little over a decade into his career as a winemaker, he had noticed that his vines seemed exhausted. No matter what treatment or intervention he used, no matter the vintage, their performance lagged. Had he done something wrong?

He took inspiration from how his wife — Sophie Countess Goëss-Enzenberg — cared for the family’s overall health. “A good diet, individual care, homeopathic remedies … We were always quite healthy.” He wondered if a similar approach could extend to his plants. Biodynamics intrigued him.

So starting in 2005, he began to adopt its principles, treating his entire property as a self-sustaining organism and eschewing chemicals. In his opinion, the results justified the radical mindset shift and even more so, the enormous effort. Of course, vineyards have a remarkable ability to recover from the brink, and their resilience can come from a variety of factors. But seeing how dense the grass and herbs were in his vine rows compared to his neighbors — a hallmark I’ve seen in biodynamic vineyards from Oregon to Alsace — made it hard for me to dismiss the sentiment.

Seeking third-party guidance and verification of his methods, he helped establish respekt-BIODYN, which is today a well-regarded certification body for biodynamic wine production that includes Foradori, Salicutti, Loimer, Tement, Wieninger and Judith Beck as its members.

The ethos of the practice prompted the family to embrace biodiversity in a big way, planting trees and shrubbery throughout the vineyards, promoting cover crops and flowers in the vine rows, and even welcoming birds (who aren’t without their problems for farmers) by building nesting boxes throughout the property.

Michael described how all of this life — from the trees and the wild birds to the chickens and sheep who graze amidst the vine rows — creates “an anima” which benefits the vines and yields fruit he’d rather work with in the cellar.

Of course, many of us in the group had heard similar biodynamic conversion stories from winegrowers. But sometimes the mindset of letting nature handle itself carries over to the cellar and leads to sloppy, unpredictable cloudy wines.

I was hoping that Michael’s tailored threads was an indication that he preferred precision in the cellar as well.

A Building with Lungs

The tan Tirolean manor house at the heart of Manincor dates back to 1608. It is surrounded by vineyards, and bears an octagonal tower that appears to be wearing the Sorting Hat from Harry Potter. Despite their noble stature and history as winemakers, the Goëss-Enzenberg family was selling its fruit off to the cooperatives of Terlano and Caldaro until the early 1990s. It was then that Michael decided to explore estate bottling. It didn’t take long for him to see that he needed a more sophisticated facility to achieve his ambitions. So rather than tarnish the heritage of the house and its verdant surroundings, Michael went underground.

Completed in 2004, Manincor’s cellar lies beneath one of the vineyards and is partially powered by geothermal energy. It is surrounded on four sides by a tall concrete hallway that acts like a moat of cool air, regulating temperature, humidity and airflow. “It’s like the lungs of the building,” Michael said, before shrugging with his eyebrows as if to say I know it’s too ugly for such poetry.

We sat down for a tasting of his recent vintages, and the wines leaped from the glass. From the Terlaner white blend to the Bordeaux blend named after his son, they were fresh yet precise, dignified yet generous in spirit. But it was my first taste of the “Mason di Mason” Pinot Noir — the 2022 — where his wines made the boldest declaration. It was easily the finest Pinot Noir I’ve tasted from Italy, and I’ve been searching for a long time. Where the hell have you been all this time? I wrote in my notes.

Across the table, a somm laughed at another for merely writing YUMMY!!!!! in all caps across the page. “How many exclamation points do you need?” she laughed.

I was feeling like many of us at the table were too proud to admit that we’d overlooked Manincor all these years. But there’s a good reason for that: only 5% of their wines are represented on the U.S. market. Even pros don’t get exposed to all the good stuff all the time.

“We prefer to sell to family-run restaurants, because they are our best customers,” Michael said, adding that 60 percent of his total production remains in Alto Adige. (For perspective, it is not unusual to hear an Italian winery say that 60 percent is exported to the United States alone).

Why such a strategy? The Goëss-Enzenberg family simply prefers to stay local with their 350,000 bottles. “We like knowing the local businesses where our wines are sold,” he stated simply, adding that he feels invested in their success as well.

His son, Kassian — who has taken over operations, but was not available to meet with us on this day due to a prior commitment — has local ambitions of his own.

“He wants to be even more farm-to-table than we already are,” Michael said, a gleam in his eye that read like a mischievous flirtation. He knew we were clamoring for these wines, and perhaps even pining for this slice of Alto Adige. Rarity can make a thing more tantalizing.

But I also wondered how they could go even further with this philosophy. You are sourcing your own oak, powering your cellar with geothermal, and selling most of your wines locally? That isn’t enough? But before I could ask the question, a realization gave me the answer. This is what sustainability actually looks like. He’d never used that word, perhaps because it is one of the most overused words in wine. But the desire had been passed down a generation. That might not sustain for a century, but it had a better chance than some manifesto stapled onto a website.

title: Chianti Classico Annata: Toward an Ideal of Freshness
summary:
12 New Releases to Please Every Palate

The post Chianti Classico Annata: Toward an Ideal of Freshness appeared first on Opening a Bottle.
content:
10 min read

As I complete my coverage of the 2026 edition of the Chianti Classico Collection, it feels appropriate to circle back on the star of the show, a singular grape like no other in Italy: Sangiovese.

This grape has endured centuries, and perhaps millennia, of ups and downs across the heart of Tuscany. Its desire for a long growing season can make it seem like a raconteur who collects stories across each vintage. Its adaptability in the cellar (sparkling, rosato, red) should never be confused with nonchalance. Sangiovese is a demanding grape, and it will offer an anonymous performance if its conditions aren’t met.

Which is what makes Chianti Classico so remarkable. Here you have a massive growing region that covers 170,000 acres of total area, with 345 producers of various size and ethos, and yet it consistently delivers on the promise of Sangiovese.

That is particularly apparent with the entry-level wines, known as Chianti Classico (or unofficially as annata, for annual). Requiring only 12 months of aging and a minimum of 80% Sangiovese, these wines are habitually fresh, juicy and pleasing. Some are tinged with savory and herbal dimensions, others offer a hint at the more serious tannins and resolute structure of the Chianti Classico Riserva and Chianti Classico Gran Selezione categories. Some do it all.

I’ve been tracking these wines for over a decade now, and I find it comforting that these wines are only getting more refreshing, more versatile and more interesting with each vintage.

It’s hard to identify a single Italian red wine that does it all. But if there is one category that does it most, it’s Chianti Classico. The following 12 stood out to me during my marathon tastings at February’s Chianti Classico Collection.

Navigate This Guide

  • Top Wine (Part 1)
  • Top Wine (Part 2)
  • Top Wine (Part 3)
  • Categorical Trifecta: Castello di Volpaia

Alphabetical

  • 2019 Antico Podere Casanova-Bucciarelli Chianti Classico
  • 2024 Belvedere1 Tenuta de’ Carfini Chianti Classico
  • 2023 Castagnoli Chianti Classico
  • 2024 Castello di Volpaia Chianti Classico
  • 2024 Cigliano di Sopra Chianti Classico
  • 2023 La Sala del Torriano Chianti Classico
  • 2023 L’Erta di Radda Chianti Classico
  • 2024 Monteraponi Chianti Classico
  • 2023 Nittardi “Vigna Doghessa” Chianti Classico
  • 2023 Rocca di Montegrossi Chianti Classico
  • 2022 San Donatino “Poggio ai Mori” Chianti Classico
  • 2023 Tenuta di Carleone Chianti Classico

Captions

Top Wine (Part 1): 2022 San Donatino “Poggio ai Mori” Chianti Classico

             

The landscape of Chianti Classico in the early 1970s was profuse with abandoned plots of land. A rural economic hangover from World War II lingered, and the collapse of the mezzadria system altered the way lend was tended. It was at this moment that many outsiders bought estates as vacation homes. But for French singer Léo Ferré and his wife Maria Cristina Diaz, their purchase of San Donatino was motivated by a desire to maintain the farm. They sold wine for bulk until the 1990s when they shifted to estate bottling. Organic certification occurred in 2012, and their son, Mathieu, oversees the estate today.

This family-owned Castellina winery amazed me with the two wines it exhibited at the Chianti Classico Collection.

Their annata arrived at my station toward the end of the second day of tasting, and it was like a lightning bolt of energy for my palate. Refreshing, yes, but impressively complex and structured, like a Gran Selezione in disguise. Its aromas were expressive and unified beautifully, so much so that I couldn’t take my nose away from the rim. I am excited to track San Donatino’s wines into future vintages.

Return to Wine List

Top Wine (Part 2): 2023 L’Erta di Radda Chianti Classico

     

Another way of looking at aromas — rather than a list of this and that — is to imagine a horizon. How wide and clear is the view?

The nose of the beautifully fresh 2023 annata from L’Erta di Radda was like an expansive view in evening light. Not only were the proper elements “within sight” — the cherries, the citrus, the herbs, the savory — but they were cast in remarkable resolution. Imagine a vista you’ve seen a hundred times before, but in a particular light you’ve never encountered. It stops you in your tracks because suddenly, everything feels new.

There was an illusion of sweetness here that was notable. Of course the wine is perfectly dry, but the cherry-like tones landed more softly than most Chianti Classico, which countered the wonderful amaro traits that often show up with Radda wines. I love how some producers can make an annata feel complex and important without being showy, and this was a prime example.

Return to Wine List

Top Wine (Part 3): 2023 Tenuta di Carleone Chianti Classico

         

If I am talking a lot about Radda in these reports, it’s because there are a ton of great producers in this centralized village. Year in year out, this UGA zone has the highest ranked wines at the Chianti Classico Collection, and it’s not even particularly close.

But Tenuta di Carleone is not just another Radda winery. There is a character here that I find engaging in an intellectual way; a wine that asks more questions than it answers. For instance, why only make one Chianti Classico? No Riserva? No Gran Selezione? (OK, that one is easy to answer: the estate is too small). More mysterious is how this wine is able to conjure up so many sensations — tangy fruit, earthiness, herbaceousness, zingy citrus, precise bitterness, sweetness as a sleight-of-hand — without feeling overbearing. Drinking this wine is like a dialogue, and its persistence isn’t so much a technical trait of acidity or alcohol, but rather an insatiable desire to see where the conversation goes.

Return to Wine List

Categorical Trifecta: 2024 Castello di Volpaia Chianti Classico

            

The one estate to appear in all three Chianti Classico Tasting Reports this year is Castello di Volpaia. Their annual release, entry-level Chianti Classico is an ideal stepping stone on a lot of levels: into their range of wines, into a true taste of Radda, into a fun progressive meal as a first-course pairing. This wine has what I call a “mountain fruit” profile, in that you can tell through its beautiful tanginess that the grapes were grown in a mountainous climate where the nights are sufficiently cool. Reds from Valle d’Aosta, the mountains of Piedmont and Alto Adige always exhibit this trait, and for me, it shows up at Castello di Volpaia too. But where things shift into classic Chianti Classico territory is in the minty dimensions to it. This is a spirited wine with ample freshness and sneaky complexity.

Return to Wine List

2024 Cigliano di Sopra Chianti Classico

     

I will admit that I was wrong.

As I noted in my report on the Chianti Classico Riserva, where Cigliano di Sopra’s “Vigneto Branca” came out on top, my personal taste has had a wrestling match with this estate’s wines for a few years. They have always struck me as jolly and expressive, but due to their lack of savory and herbal elements, they didn’t feel like Chianti Classico to me.

But I came to realize at this edition, that Maddelena Fucile and Matteo Vaccari’s interpretation of San Casciano in Val di Pesa — their home terroir within Chianti Classico — is indeed as faithful as they come. They’re certified organic, low intervention in ethos, and they also embrace whole-cluster fermentation when they deem it appropriate. That can misdirect the senses a bit. But the soil of their vineyard is more pebbly, and the growing season of San Casciano in Val di Pesa is shorter, so producing something that is robust and structured can feel forced. That makes me feel like their approach is amply justified.

With their 2024 annata, everything comes together beautifully with red tea aromas, a touch of bitter orange, and the happiest of dispositions on the palate. This is a great summer red wine if you find it.

Return to Wine List

2023 La Sala del Torriano Chianti Classico

             

If Cigliano di Sopra renders San Casciano in Val di Pesa on the canvas with watercolors, then La Sala del Torriano sketches it with charcoal. Both estates have a wonderful minerality and persistence to their wines, which feels like a common trait of the zone, but La Sala del Torriano’s wines allow for a more brooding disposition.

“Mocha and macchia” I wrote in my notes for the 2023 Chianti Classico, shorthand for an espresso-cocoa like bitterness that mingles with the catch-all Tuscan term for dried herbs. But again, it is the length of this wine that dazzled the most.

Return to Wine List

2019 Antico Podere Casanova-Bucciarelli Chianti Classico

             

The Bucciarelli family has sharecropping roots and they have presided over this estate since 1926. But one of the previous owners (in 1549) was Michelangelo. Make of that what you will. For me, tasting wine from an estate like this feels like participation in history, and it is one of Chianti Classico’s great intangibles.

But the wines have to be good, of course, and fortunately with Bucciarelli they always are.

There is an old-school Chianti sensibility to their entry-level wine. For one, it is released extraordinarily late: the 2019 is the current release of the entry-level wine. (The latest Riserva is a 2017!). And while this wine is 100% Sangiovese, it seems to have some of those traits of yesteryear, back when Chianti Classico was always a blended wine: there is plenty of leather, citrus, and dried cherries, but moreover, lower acidity and subdued tannins. This is product of the aging of course, and for some, this wine might be held back for too long. But here is your chance to taste Chianti Classico at a different stage, and to me, it is undeniably compelling.

Return to Wine List

2023 Nittardi “Vigna Doghessa” Chianti Classico

             

Whereas Bucciarelli was once the estate of Michelangelo, so too was Nittardi. The estates were once a cohesive whole, and today they reside “next door” to each other. Of course, in Chianti Classico terms, that means a small forest separates the two.

Unusually for the region, Nittardi chooses to make its single-vineyard Vigna Doghessa as an annata, which is countered by a second, more fruity annata called “Belcanto.” Meanwhile, the Gran Selezione is truly a selection of the year’s best fruit. The 2023 Vigna Doghessa holds back a bit on the fruit because of its extraordinary savoriness, which emerges immediately and acts as a unifying element of the wine’s flavor. Polished tannins and a measured demeanor make this wine a special pick for home chefs looking for a versatile pairing partner.

Return to Wine List

2024 Monteraponi Chianti Classico

       

To some, Monteraponi’s status as one of Tuscany’s greatest estates is established by the prices its top wines fetch. (Hmm, they must be good). For me, it is a resignation that I have to include them in these reports. Despite my desire to spread the wealth and highlight as many new discoveries as possible, Monteraponi keeps towering over its peers in terms of finesse and confidence. If you taste enough Chianti Classico, you’ll feel the difference when Monteraponi dashes across the palate. For me, it registers on the herbaceous level: rather than rosemary or minty, it seems like a sweet tobacco.

The good news is that the standard bearer annata conveys this Monteraponi-ness while remaining within reach of most budgets: a sub-$50 wine that tastes like it’s more than a C-note.

Return to Wine List

2023 Rocca di Montegrossi Chianti Classico

           

Rocca di Montegrossi’s wines are best suited to wine drinkers who prize power yet appreciate finesse. Somehow, those traits are never mutually exclusive at this Gaiole-based estate, even in their entry-level wine. The 2023 annata has a spirited and muscular sense of self-confidence with momentous acidity and eager tannins that are tempered by fruit reminiscent of sweet black cherries and blood orange. Despite its stature and intensity, the wine functions as a highly versatile player when it comes to pairings. Don’t be shy experimenting with it.

Return to Wine List

2023 Castagnoli Chianti Classico

           

Hopefully, these Chianti Classico tasting reports give you a sense for the spectrum of flavors and sensations available in this varied terroir. The more I get to know it, the more I marvel at this trait.

For instance, while tasting Castagnoli’s 2023 Chianti Classico, I felt a fruity character that often shows up in southern Italian reds like Aglianico and Nero d’Avola. Call it what you will — sun-dried, baked or just “ripe” — but at Castagnoli it never comes across as “cooked.” Rather it is simply a feature of vineyards that felt ample warmth throughout the vintage. I love how the identity of this region remains despite this “southern” characteristic, lending yet another perspective on this multi-faceted appellation.

Return to Wine List

2024 Belvedere1 “Tenuta de’ Carfini” Chianti Classico

         

We finish with a newcomer to Chianti Classico: Belvedere1. Don’t let the “luxury wellness retreat” marketing of the estate dilute your opinion of what’s going on with the wines, because they’re actually quite true to form for Chianti Classico, and effortlessly delicious.

There are two annata wines, for now: this wine, and a standard annata which is very fruity. One can imagine that they have Gran Selezione designs for this single-vineyard wine, as it takes a few years to meet those parameters. Tenuta de’ Carfini is far more expressive than the entry-level wine, with a wonderful stoniness to it that lends persistence.

Return to Wine List

Captions

From top to bottom (All photos ©Kevin Day/Opening a Bottle):

  • Members of the Associazione Italiana Sommelier (AIS) keep the Chianti Classico Collection running smoothly, and they are the true lifeblood of the event;
  • One of this year’s top annata wines: 2022 San Donatino “Poggio ai Mori” Chianti Classico;
  • Not too dark, not too light — this is the right color of an annata Chianti Classico;
  • The 2023 L’Erta di Radda Chianti Classico is a wine we should celebrate for its brightness and vivacity;
  • Evening passeggiata by the closed storefronts on the Ponte Vecchio in Florence;
  • The 2023 “Vigna Doghessa” Chianti Classico from Nittardi, a winery that often appears in this report;
  • Newcomer Belvedere1 made their mark with this “Tenuta de’ Carfini” Chianti Classico, which likely be given the Gran Selezione treatment as the winery’s operation matures;
  • The 2023 Castagnoli Chianti Classico;
  • Visiting the famed Monteraponi estate in Radda.

Note: I was invited to attend the Chianti Classico Collection as a member of the media as part of the annual Anteprima di Toscana. My travel and accommodations to and from Florence were covered. Learn more about our editorial policy.

title: Chianti Classico Riserva: Don’t Sleep On This Category
summary:
10 Wines to Buy and Drink Now (Or Cellar For the Years Ahead)

The post Chianti Classico Riserva: Don’t Sleep On This Category appeared first on Opening a Bottle.
content:
10 min read

So if the Gran Selezione category has achieved its grand vision, what exactly is the vision for Chianti Classico Riserva?

When well made, Chianti Classico Riserva can be every bit as thrilling and interesting as Gran Selezione, yet often more affordable.

Last year, I asked whether the context for serving a Riserva was any different than Gran Selezione, ultimately deducing that it is roughly the same. Efforts to explain these categories with pyramids will always be a disservice to what Riserva can be, because the implication is that it is “second best.” The truth is that several of my favorite Chianti Classico producers file their top wine as Riserva and don’t (or just can’t) put it in the Gran Selezione bucket.

There are two reasons a winemaker might stick with only a Riserva:

  • The rules — Their philosophy espouses Chianti Classico as a blended wine — with more than 10% of the blend being indigenous grapes or any of the sanctioned international grapes not allowable under the Gran Selezione;
  • Their business – Or, most pertinently and most commonly, their business can’t quite accommodate the regulations of Gran Selezione.

This last attribute could mean many things, such as the producer isn’t entirely using estate-owned grapes (a big one, and required for Gran Selezione), or they just don’t have the means to store wines for another six months of unrealized revenue.

Do these attributes indicate lesser quality? Not at all. In fact, for a consumer, they represent opportunity. When well made, Chianti Classico Riserva can be every bit as thrilling and interesting as Gran Selezione, yet often more affordable.

During my two days of tasting at the Chianti Classico Collection, the one spot where Riserva seemed muddled was when it felt lost in the portfolio of a producer making all three categories. The Annata represents the entry point to the house style, the Gran Selezione gives us the most focused and terroir-centric expression, and the Riserva in between is … what exactly? In a few occasions, it wasn’t exactly clear. Do we need this bridge between point A and B?

Not always. As standalone wines, Riserva are often well made and technically correct, but their narrative can sometimes leave you asking why is this here?

However, of the wines I tasted, there were dozens that had plenty of answers. The following rose to the top and suggested that the category is still vital, even if it requires a bit of explaining.

Be sure to also read my Gran Selezione and Annata reports.

Navigate This Guide

  • Top Wine
  • Most Elegant
  • Cellar Pick
  • Hidden Gem
  • Most Old School

Alphabetical

  • 2021 Badia a Coltibuono Chianti Classico Riserva
  • 2022 Castagnoli “Terrazze” Chianti Classico Riserva
  • 2023 Castello di Volpaia Chianti Classico Riserva
  • 2023 Cigliano di Sopra “Vigneto Branca” Chianti Classico Riserva
  • 2023 Il Molino di Grace Chianti Classico Riserva
  • 2023 La Vigna di San Marino ad Argiano Chianti Classico Riserva
  • 2022 L’Erta di Radda Chianti Classico Riserva
  • 2020 Losi Querciavalle Chianti Classico Riserva
  • 2022 Poggerino “Bugialla” Chianti Classico Riserva
  • 2019 San Donatino “Poggio Ai Mori” Chianti Classico Riserva

Top Wine: 2023 Cigliano di Sopra “Vigneto Branca” Chianti Classico Riserva

     

Maddelena Fucile and Matteo Vaccari of Cigliano di Sopra are among an exciting group of young producers who are energizing Chianti Classico with their open minds. They started in 2016, and have steadily evolved towards finer and finer expressions of their San Casciano in Val di Pesa terroir. I’ve been tracking their wines since we met at the estate in 2022. Their enthusiasm, curiosity and zest made it one of the more memorable visits of my time in the area.

As noted in the Gran Selezione report, whole cluster fermentation is becoming a fashionable technique in the zone, partly because of this couple’s success with it. But last year, while tasting their wines, I was reminded a bit too much of Beaujolais Cru (“violets, candied cherries, peppercorn”), which to me, didn’t feel very Gallo Nero (“rosemary, blood orange, black cherry”). But at this year’s edition of the Chianti Classico Collection, those two wavelengths harmonized brilliantly in the “Vigneto Branca,” and I found myself saying oh, I get it now.

This is a Chianti Classico whose preferred tool isn’t a hammer, but rather a needle and thread. It has an exceptional sense for flowers on the nose, which registers as a playfulness that is not often associated with Riserva. But on the palate, the area’s signature fruit and soft leather sensation emerges (pure Chianti Classico), which rides a wave of acidity I can only describe as refreshing. One can imagine this wine “graduating” to Gran Selezione someday given its specific sense of vineyard identity. Doesn’t matter to me. For now, I’m perfectly happy buying it as a Riserva.

Return to Wine List

Most Elegant: 2022 L’Erta di Radda Chianti Classico Riserva

     

One trait that should emerge with all Riserva wines is a sense of depth. This can come in the form of complexity, but more often than not, it arrives with a general sense of richness. Fruit that feels darker and more weighty, alcohol that seems to tinge things sweeter.

L’Erta di Radda’s 2022 Riserva demonstrates this perfectly, especially when tasted alongside the estate’s brilliant standard-bearer annata. But what makes both wines magical is that they have the proper profile of their origin, Radda in Chianti, a place where Sangiovese regularly achieves its highest potential of finesse. The acidity feels silkier here, the tannins more fine but no-less active. Winemaker Diego Finocchi has roots in the village but not winemaking, yet he started this winery in 2006 at the age of 24 with a mere 5 hectares of tough, steep vineyard. Such a story seems impossible today given land values, but his drive and energy have propelled his wines to the forefront for Chianti Classico watchers. The 2022 Riserva is simply gorgeous: black fruit, leather and swirls of dried herbs punctuate its flavors, while a lingering persistence feels like an invitation for another sip.

Return to Wine List

Cellar Pick: 2020 Losi Querciavalle Chianti Classico Riserva

         

In the southern stretches of Chianti Classico, in the UGA of Castelnuovo Berardenga, you not only get whispers of the Mediterranean on the breeze, you feel it in the wines. What might be taken for granted in Montalcino is seen as a blessing here: wines with a sun-dried quality to the fruit, supreme swaths of citrus, and an iron-like aroma that feels firm and durable. Fèlsina is the most famous estate in these parts, but for the last three Chianti Classico Collections, I’ve taken notice of Losi Querciavalle as the leading indicator of Castelnuovo Berardenga terroir. This year, it was more noticeable and appreciated in the Riserva than the “Millennium” Gran Selezione. A compelling sanguine quality to its flavors made it feel resilient while muscular alcohol and a salty persistence allowed it to linger powerfully on the finish.

Return to Wine List

Hidden Gem: 2022 Castagnoli “Terrazze” Chianti Classico Riserva

           

The wines of Castagnoli first caught my attention last fall in Denver at the Festa del Chianti Classico. There was something about their purity of spirit that made them standout in a crowded walk-around tasting that had turned into a bustling social hour. I wanted to go back for more (always a good sign in a setting where, let’s be honest, it is hard to focus).

As the name of the Riserva suggests, the vines for this wine are beautiful terraced with olive trees integrated here and there — a sight that reminds me of Gravner’s vineyards up in Friuli. The wine is aged in neutral French barriques, which seems to give it a wonderful openness from the start. Even though this is a Castellina wine, fans of those from Radda will be quiet pleased here, as it is deftly mineral and lifted on the palate: cherries, rosemary sprigs and the warmth of the Mediterranean came to mind. This is clearly a wine that gets ample sun, but doesn’t feel stewed or baked in any way. Just effortlessly elegant.

Return to Wine List

Most Old School: 2021 Badia a Coltibuono Chianti Classico Riserva

          

The spirit of a wine is an elusive thing to describe, which is partly what makes calling a wine “old school” problematic. But you know it when you feel it in a lineup like this. Imagine an audition where every actor coming through the door is eager and energetic, and then in walks someone with experience, command and wisdom. How do you know its wisdom? It’s not a look or an action. It’s a feeling. You give them the part.

That’s a rather round-about way of describing what this Chianti Classico Riserva from Badia a Coltibuono conveyed to me. The leathery-savory aromas could only come from Sangiovese grown in Tuscany. Not only that, the wine’s profile on the palate suggested it could only come from an experienced hand, given how patient yet nuanced the interplay of acidity, tannin and flavor was.

Roberto Stucchi has been in charge of the winemaking at Badia a Coltibuono since 1985. For a winery of this size (up to 350,000 bottles per year) he remains a rare holdout from the Gran Selezione category. Instead he makes two Riserva: one in oak casks, and another called “Cultus” in barriques. The latter could never be a Gran Selezione because 20% of the blend is indigenous local red grapes, whose legacy Roberto is passionate about preserving. This Riserva is comprised of a 10% mix of Colorino, Canaiolo and Ciliegiolo, which seem to counter Sangiovese’s cherry-orange-savory notes with a seedy raspberry note that lends freshness.

Return to Wine List

2022 Poggerino “Bugialla” Chianti Classico Riserva

.      

I do love a classic wine — one you can point to with a newcomer and say “taste that wine … that’s the baseline of what this place is all about.”

Poggerino’s “Bugialla” is often that wine, and once again it asserted itself at this year’s edition of the Chianti Classico Collection. Piero Lanza is a bit of a Sangiovese whsiperer, and this wine’s patient personality and clearly etched lines reminded me of his meticulous personality. The wine offers that emblematic nose of Chianti Classico: soft black cherries, salumi, rosemary and orange peel. On the palate, “Bugialla’s” exceptional balance and citric zing feels comforting in its familiarity. This vineyard comes from near the geographic center of the DOCG, which feels appropriate for a wine this representative of the entire place.

Return to Wine List

2023 La Vigna di San Martino ad Argiano Chianti Classico Riserva

       

La Vigna di San Martino is the work of enologist Giampaolo Chiettini, who used to work at Isole e Olena. As noted in the Gran Selezione report, the vines that he works with are in San Casciano in Val di Pesa and owned by the bishopric of Florence.

This wine had a startling set of aromas that made it stand out in the crowd: the fruit seemed dried yet still lively and fresh, and there was a dual note of cinnamon and cedar piercing through the aromas that was very intriguing. Despite that dried fruit tone, it did not feel prematurely aged: coarse cotton tannins and superb persistence suggested interesting times ahead for this wine.

Return to Wine List

2019 San Donatino “Poggio Ai Mori” Chianti Classico Riserva

             

Here was a Riserva with a little more age than the rest. For San Donatino, the Riserva is not an every year wine — only in the best due they produce it. And even then, they far exceed the minimum requirements on aging with 48 months (instead of 24) in large 30hL Slavonian oak casks followed by even more bottle age.

So here we are with this one: it’s in its prime and ready to be uncorked. There is a timelessness to the aromas here: cherries, orange bitters, juniper and leather feel woven together seamlessly, as well as profound wooly tannins. This is what Tuscan Sangiovese in its prime feels like.

Return to Wine List

2023 Il Molino di Grace Chianti Classico Riserva

       

Like Poggerino’s “Bugialla,” Il Molino di Grace puts the classic in Chianti Classico, as it had near-perfect typicity on the nose: pure cherry-like fruit, soft orange peel, soft leather and an herbaceousness recalling spearmint. This is what Panzano, the UGA from which this wine hails from, does best: it hits the Chianti Classico middle ground with such clarity and purpose.

On the palate, the 2023 had wonderful richness and depth. While it is a very straightforward example of Chianti Classico Riserva, it delivers its remarks so precisely that I comes across as a standout.

Return to Wine List

2023 Castello di Volpaia Chianti Classico Riserva

           

Castello di Volpaia is one of a small handful of estates that each year make all three categories make sense. The Chianti Classico annata is playful, versatile and lean-bodied, while the estate’s two Gran Selezione — Coltassala and Il Puro — are genuine celebrations of site specifics.

Residing between them is the Riserva, a wine of consistency and depth, which in 2023 projects the aromatic and mineral charms of Radda beautifully with tea-like tannins. Across all three, the winery’s style and “Radda-ness” are consistent, but it is the elegance of the Riserva that perhaps makes it my favorite wine from them each year.

Return to Wine List

Captions

From top to bottom (All photos ©Kevin Day/Opening a Bottle):

  • This year’s top riserva: Cigliano di Sopra’s 2023 “Vigneto Branca” Chianti Classico Riserva;
  • Maddalena Fucile and Matteo Vaccari at Cigliano di Sopra wine estate in San Casciano in Val di Pesa;
  • The ornate door to the Cappella Pazzi at Basilica di Santa Croce in Florence;
  • Unearthing treasures at the Chianti Classico Collection, like this 2020 Losi Querciavalle Chianti Classico Riserva;
  • Another unexpected treasure: the 2023 La Vigna di San Marino ad Argiano Chianti Classico Riserva;
  • The 2022 “Bugialla” from Poggerino was showing beautifully;
  • Sommeliers must walk five or six miles each day traversing the long train platforms of Stazione Leopolda;
  • My view as the first of 60 Riserva wines was poured … gotta get the shot while the glasses are at their cleanest;
  • Sunday afternoon in Piazza Santa Croce in Florence.
